data.title: Fed's Waller says labor market is weaker than it looks, justifying interest-rate cut this month
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Private-sector job growth is near 'stall speed and flashing red,' Fed governor says

Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller on Thursday laid out the argument he is going to make in two weeks to his central bank colleagues - cut interest rates now because the labor market is weaker than you think, and inflation from higher tariffs isn't a worry.

"The economy is still growing but its momentum has slowed significantly, and the risks to the FOMC's employment mandate have increased," Waller said in a speech to the Money Marketeers of New York University, a group of economists who closely follow the bond market.

Waller is on President Donald Trump's short list to replace Jerome Powell as Fed chair next year, so some analysts think political considerations are behind his call for easing so soon.

Over the past six weeks, Fed Governor Michelle Bowman is the only other one of the 19 top Fed officials to back a July cut. Waller and Bowman are the only two Fed governors appointed by Trump to the Fed board during his first term.

Trump has urged the Fed to slash rates by as much as three percentage points. However, the president doesn't argue that the economy is weakening and needs the cuts, as Waller does.

Traders in derivative markets see a minimal chance of a rate cut at the Fed's meeting on July 29-30.

In their speeches over the past six weeks, most Fed officials have said they want to wait and see how inflation data looks over the summer before cutting rates. In general, Fed officials have described the labor market as remaining healthy.

On Wednesday, New York Fed President John Williams said delaying any hike until there was more information about inflation "was entirely appropriate." He said the most recent numbers "show a solid labor market."

It is true that job growth has slowed, but so has labor supply, with lower immigration. So overall the labor market is well balanced, Williams said.

The New York Fed president, who is part of Powell's inner circle, also said tariffs are pushing up inflation and there will be even higher prices for consumers in coming months.

Waller disagreed.

In his speech Thursday, he said "tariffs are one-off increases in the price level and do not cause inflation beyond a temporary surge." That means that there might be some higher prices but not a continuing upward spiral.

Waller said it looks like a large share of the tariffs, which are taxes on imported goods, won't be paid by consumers. He said that foreign suppliers and U.S. importers would split most of the bill.

Data from Fed staff indicate that if you subtract tariff effects from reported inflation data, underlying inflation is close to the Fed's 2% target, he added.

Waller noted that the private sector only added 74,000 jobs in June, the slowest pace since October 2024. He said that increase may be exaggerated, noting that there have been downward revisions in recent years.

"This is why I say private-sector payroll gains are near stall speed and flashing red," he said.

Waller said it would be better for the Fed to cut sooner rather than later. That would keep the Fed from falling behind if the economy weakens in August and September. And if the Fed did cut rates and the data later pointed to the need for fewer cuts, the Fed could hold their policy steady for one or more meetings.

Waller added he would support another quarter-point cut later this year if inflation remains in check and the economy grows slowly.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Livestock Improvement Posts Higher Fiscal 2025 Earnings as Revenue Rises; Lifts Dividend
data.description:
Livestock Improvement reported Friday fiscal 2025 underlying earnings of NZ$0.15 per share, up from NZ$0.10 per share in the year-earlier period.

Revenue for the year ended May 31 was NZ$295.1 million, up from NZ$267.3 million a year earlier.

The board declared a final dividend of NZ$0.122 per share, compared with NZ$0.058 a year earlier, payable on Aug. 15 to shareholders of record as of Aug 1.

New Zealand Stocks Dip But Head for Weekly Gains
data.description:
New Zealand shares fell 80 points or 0.6% to 12,825 in early trade on Friday, snapping a three-day winning streak as losses in logistics, healthcare, and transport weighed.

The market pulled back from a five-month high as investors booked profits.

Sentiment was also dampened by signs of rising cost pressures, with annual food inflation hitting an 18-month high in June.

Traders turned cautious ahead of key domestic data next week, including Q2 consumer inflation and the June trade balance.

Among the top drags were Restaurant Brands NZ (-2.9%), Delegat Group (-1.7%), Serko (-1.0%), and Skellerup (-0.8%).

Still, the NZX 50 is poised to end the week higher, up roughly 1% so far, rebounding from recent weakness.

Gains earlier in the week were supported by record highs on Wall Street after U.S. President Trump said he had no plans to remove Fed Chair Powell.

Meanwhile, China, New Zealand’s largest trading partner, pledged to boost domestic consumption and curb the EV price war.

data.title: BHP's fourth-quarter iron ore output rises 1%
data.description:
BHP Group on Friday reported a 1% rise in fourth-quarter iron ore output, helped by supply chain strength at its central Pilbara hub in Western Australia.

The world's largest listed miner said iron ore production from those operations on a 100% basis was 77.5 million metric tonnes (Mt) in the three months ended June 30, up from 76.8 Mt a year earlier.

That beat a Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 75.90 Mt.

Lawyers in banking cases take a loss despite $35 million fee award
data.description:
Two prominent U.S. law firms that said they invested more than $100 million in attorney time litigating a complex antitrust case against major banks were awarded $35 million in legal fees on Thursday by a judge in Manhattan.

U.S. District Judge J. Paul Oetken’s order approved the full amount in fees that law firms Cohen Milstein and Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan sought for their work in the litigation, which netted $71 million in settlements. The judge also separately granted $23 million in expenses requested by the firms.

In their fee request in April, the firms said receiving the requested amount would still leave them with what they described as “substantial losses.”

Cohen Milstein and Quinn Emanuel represented investors who accused Credit Suisse, Bank of America , Goldman Sachs , JPMorgan Chase and other banks of conspiring to rig the multitrillion-dollar market for interest rate swaps.

Lead attorneys for the investors had no immediate comment.

Bank of America, JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs and Credit Suisse, now part of UBS, declined to comment. The banks and other defendants denied any wrongdoing.

In 2022, Credit Suisse agreed to pay $25 million to settle investors' claims.

Oetken in December 2023 refused to certify the investors’ lawsuit as a class action, limiting the potential damages in the case.

The investors’ appeal was pending when they struck a final settlement with the remaining banks for a combined $46 million to resolve the claims against them.

The law firms told Oetken that $35 million was “a small fraction of the huge amounts that class counsel invested in this case.”

In his fee order, Oetken said that there was a significant risk the investors would have recovered less or nothing at all without the settlements.

The case is In re: Interest Rate Swaps Antitrust Litigation, U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York, No. 16-md-02704.

US judge rejects lawyers' $94 million fee bid in auto parts pricing case

Lawyers defend $205 million legal fee in US auto class action settlement

data.title: Vir Biotechnology EVP Sells Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameEisner Mark
RelationshipEVP and Chief Medical Officer
TypeSell
Amount$56,790
SEC FilingForm 4

Mark Eisner, EVP and Chief Medical Officer of Vir Biotechnology, sold a total of 10,382 shares of common stock on July 15 and July 17, 2025, for a total sale amount of $56,790. The sales were conducted at weighted average prices of $5.4682 and $5.4738, respectively. Following these transactions, Eisner directly owns 104,618 shares of Vir Biotechnology. The sales on July 15 were to cover tax obligations related to restricted stock units, and the sales on July 17 were pursuant to a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on August 5, 2024.

SEC Filing: Vir Biotechnology, Inc. [ VIR ] - Form 4 - Jul. 17, 2025

data.title: Genesis Minerals Target Price Raised 2.5% to A$4.10/Share by Shaw & Partners>GMD.AU
Qantas Airways Faces Class Action Over Customer Data Breach
data.description:
Qantas Airways is facing a class action lawsuit filed by Maurice Blackburn with the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner, the law firm's website showed.

The class action alleges that the airline breached privacy laws by failing to safeguard the data of millions of customers, including contact details, birth dates, and Frequent Flyer numbers.

Qantas did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.
